To find the number of practice sessions that maximizes the average batting score, we analyze the quadratic equation \( S = 50 + 5n - 0.2n^2 \). The maximum value of a quadratic function \( ax^2 + bx + c \) occurs at \( n = -b / 2a \). Here, \( a = -0.2 \) and \( b = 5 \). Substituting these values gives \( n = -5 / (2 \times -0.2) = -5 / -0.4 = 12.5 \). Since the number of sessions must be an integer, we check \( n = 12 \) and \( n = 13 \). For \( n = 12 \), \( S = 50 + 60 - 28.8 = 81.2 \). For \( n = 13 \), \( S = 50 + 65 - 33.8 = 81.2 \). Both yield the same maximum score. Thus, 12 or 13 sessions maximize the score at 81.2.

Next, we calculate the change in win probability when sessions increase from 5 to 10. First, find \( S \) for \( n = 5 \): \( S_5 = 50 + 25 - 5 = 70 \). Then find \( S \) for \( n = 10 \): \( S_{10} = 50 + 50 - 20 = 80 \). Using the logistic function \( P = 1 / (1 + e^{-(0.1S - 3)}) \), we calculate \( P_5 \). The exponent is \( -(0.1 \times 70 - 3) = -(7 - 3) = -4 \). So \( P_5 = 1 / (1 + e^{-4}) \approx 1 / (1 + 0.0183) \approx 0.982 \).
